_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Patients who are stable with no bowel compromise
Patients who are hemodynamically unstable without bowel compromise (caecopexy may be performed in conjunction with a caecostomy tube placement or caecopexy alone)

preauth.label: Clinical notes including evaluation findings and planned line of treatment

ppd_preauth.q: Did CT abdominopelvic scan confirm the diagnosis?
Was the clinical presentation and imaging indicative of surgery?

cpd_claim.q: Was CT scan report indicative of surgery?

common_queries: Clinical presentation and imaging are not indicative of surgery for caecal volvulus.
CT abdominopelvic scan did not confirm the diagnosis of caecal volvulus.
Detailed clinical notes, including the indication for procedure, planned line of treatment, or advice for admission, are missing or incomplete.
Detailed operative/procedure notes are missing or do not adequately detail surgical steps.
Discharge summary is missing or lacks follow-up advice at the time of discharge.

clinical_key_pointers: Volvulus occurs when portions of the bowel get entangled upon a mesenteric axis, causing impairment of blood supply or obstruction of the bowel lumen, due to redundant and loose mesentery twisting.
Caecal volvulus involves the terminal ileum and right colon.
Associated risk factors include advanced age, chronic constipation, and diets rich in high fibre.
Caecal volvulus most commonly occurs in the second and third decade of life.

Three types of caecal volvulus exist: Type 1 (clockwise axial twisting, right lower quadrant), Type 2 (twisting of cecum and terminal ileum, displaced to ectopic location), and Type 3 (caecal bascule, upward folding without axial twisting).
Type 1 and Type 2 account for approximately 80% of all caecal volvuli.
Clinical presentation often involves intestinal obstruction, acute onset severe abdominal pain, constipation, nausea, vomiting, and a tympanitic, markedly distended abdomen.
Evaluation includes complete blood count (CBC) with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, and lactic acid.
Radiographic imaging (abdominal plain films, CT scan) helps differentiate from other pathologies; CT scan is diagnostic for caecal volvulus in ~90% of patients.
A mesentery torsion around the ileocolic vessels (whirl sign) on CT scan is pathognomonic for caecal volvulus.
Approximately 10% of caecal volvulus cases are diagnosed intraoperatively.
surgical_options: Non-operative reduction (barium enema or colonoscopy) may be offered for caecal volvulus.
Surgical treatment varies based on patient stability and intraoperative findings.
For patients stable with bowel compromise, a right hemicolectomy or ileocolic resection followed by an ileocolic anastomosis.
For patients unstable with bowel compromise, a right hemicolectomy or ileocolic resection with an ileostomy creation, with later reversal after stabilization.
